concise:
Ayah 1–16 · linked video: 01. Al-Fajr (Ayah 1-16)

Surah Al-Fajr: Commentary on Ayahs 1-16

Ayah 1

وَٱلْفَجْرِ

By the dawn

Allah ﷻ begins this remarkable Surah with a series of oaths, and the first is by ٱلْفَجْرِ – the breaking of dawn. This word carries profound significance in Arabic. ٱلْفَجْرِ comes from the root meaning an explosion or bursting forth, similar to how rocks might burst open to gush water. It captures that moment when darkness tears open and light comes pouring through in the morning. This is the time when the night of darkness gives way to the illumination of a new day.

For the believer, ٱلْفَجْرِ represents the most challenging yet most rewarding time of prayer. It is when the نفس (soul) is at its weakest, when the struggle to rise is greatest, yet it is precisely this time when the believer draws closest to Allah ﷻ. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that this is when the door of mercy is most open, when supplication is most readily accepted.

Ayah 2

وَلَيَالٍ عَشْرٍ

And by the ten nights

The second oath is by ten nights, though they are not immediately specified. Understanding which ten nights these are requires us to examine the context and purpose of the Surah. While there have been numerous scholarly opinions about this – some suggesting the last ten nights of Ramadan, others proposing different interpretations – the evidence points most compellingly to the first ten days of Dhul-Hijjah, culminating in the day of sacrifice.

This interpretation aligns with the spiritual journey of Hajj, where these ten days represent a period of intense spiritual struggle and preparation. They are days when the pilgrim strips away all worldly attachments and status, coming before Allah ﷻ in a state of complete humility and submission. These nights are filled with worship, reflection and the building anticipation of standing on Arafat – the greatest day of the year.

Ayah 3

وَٱلشَّفْعِ وَٱلْوَتْرِ

And by the even and the odd

Allah ﷻ now swears by ٱلشَّفْعِ and ٱلْوَتْرِ – the even and the odd. ٱلشَّفْعِ refers to things that come in pairs: two, four, six, eight. ٱلْوَتْرِ refers to that which is singular, alone, odd-numbered: one, three, five, seven, nine.

Within the context of Hajj, this can be understood as referring to the days that follow the main rituals. The tradition among the Arabs, maintained in Islamic practice, is to remain in Mina for at least two additional days after the day of sacrifice, with the option of a third day. Thus we have the even (two days) and potentially the odd (three days). Some scholars have also interpreted this as the even-numbered days of Hajj contrasted with the ninth day – the day of Arafat – which stands out as singularly significant, the odd one that carries special weight among all the days.

The pairing of even and odd may also point to a deeper spiritual reality: that Allah ﷻ has created most things in pairs, while He alone is One, singular, without partner or equal.

Ayah 4

وَٱلَّيْلِ إِذَا يَسْرِ

And by the night when it passes

The fourth oath is by the night that passes or moves away. The verb يَسْرِ carries the meaning of something moving swiftly, penetrating through, making its way forward. It’s as though the night travels through time rapidly, not lingering but passing on.

In the context of Hajj, this refers to the night spent at Muzdalifah – the longest and most exhausting night of the pilgrimage. It is the night between Arafat and the day of sacrifice, when pilgrims sleep under the open sky with nothing but rocks beneath them. Yet this difficult night passes quickly, almost as if Allah ﷻ in His mercy makes it breeze by so the pilgrims can reach the relief of morning. When that dawn breaks (returning us to the first oath), it marks the beginning of a new life, as if the pilgrim has been reborn.

Ayah 5

هَلْ فِى ذَٰلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِّذِى حِجْرٍ

Is there not in [all] that an oath for one of perception?

After these four powerful oaths, Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question: Isn’t this enough of an oath for those who possess حِجْرٍ? The word حِجْرٍ is profoundly significant. It comes from the same root as Hajar (stone) and Hajj itself. حِجْرٍ refers to restraint, prevention, the ability to create a barrier that stops something from passing through.

This is one of the Arabic words for intellect, and the choice is telling. The intellect is called حِجْرٍ because it creates barriers – it restrains your emotions, your temper, your greed, your lusts and your base desires. These animal impulses within us have no natural limits; they are like wild stallions that want to run free without any control. The intellect – the حِجْرٍ – is what stops them, restrains them, puts them in check.

This is precisely the purpose of Hajj. The entire pilgrimage is designed to teach restraint and self-control. You restrain yourself from arguing (no rafath), from sin, from worldly comforts, from your normal way of life. You learn to control the wild horses within you. Allah ﷻ is essentially asking the Quraysh: This ritual that you have been given, this incredible gift of Hajj that teaches you restraint and self-control – isn’t this enough of a sign for people who actually use their intellect?

The question carries an implicit criticism: You have been given this remarkable tool to develop حِجْرٍ, to learn self-control and humility before Allah ﷻ, yet you have failed to understand its true purpose. You perform the rituals but miss the spirit entirely.

Ayah 6

أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِعَادٍ

Have you not considered how your Lord dealt with ’Aad?

Now the Surah takes what seems like a sudden turn, but it is intimately connected to what came before. Allah ﷻ says: Haven’t you seen? Haven’t you pondered? Haven’t you considered how your Master dealt with the nation of ’Aad?

The connection is clear: You were given a way to control yourselves, to develop restraint through Hajj. But there were nations before you who failed to control themselves, who let their egos run wild. Look at what happened to them.

The people of ’Aad were an ancient and powerful Arab nation, descendants of the prophet Hud (AS). Allah ﷻ describes in other parts of the Quran how He destroyed them with a fierce, roaring wind – seven nights and eight days of devastating storms that left them looking like hollow trunks of palm trees. They were compared to palm trees because they were physically massive and powerful people. But all their strength meant nothing when Allah’s punishment came.

Ayah 7

إِرَمَ ذَاتِ ٱلْعِمَادِ

[Who were] Iram of the pillars

Allah ﷻ gives us more detail about this nation: إِرَمَ the possessor of great pillars or columns. The word ٱلْعِمَادِ refers to the massive pillars and columns that defined their architecture and civilization.

إِرَمَ could refer to several things: the name of their ancestor, the name of their capital city, the name of their greatest king, or the name of their elite ruling tribe (similar to how Quraysh ruled in Mecca). Regardless of the specific reference, the point is clear: these were people known for their architectural prowess, for building structures with columns so impressive that they became their defining characteristic.

The Arabs who heard this Surah would have been familiar with the massive ruins of ’Aad’s civilization. They would pass by these ancient, towering columns during their travels and be humbled by their scale. These weren’t just buildings – they were monuments to human ambition and pride, standing as testimony to what that nation had achieved.

This mention of architectural achievement is significant because throughout this Surah, Allah ﷻ repeatedly references buildings, construction and great structures. This is not incidental – it is central to understanding the spiritual message being conveyed.

Ayah 8

ٱلَّتِى لَمْ يُخْلَقْ مِثْلُهَا فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ

The likes of whom had never been created in the land

Allah ﷻ emphasizes that nothing like them had ever been created in any of the lands. This has a dual meaning: First, they built the kinds of columns and structures that had never been seen before in the Arab lands. They were architecturally more advanced than anyone around them. Second, they themselves were physically more capable – genetically stronger, taller and more imposing than other peoples. Nobody had seen anything like them.

This description serves a purpose for the Quraysh listening to these words. Allah ﷻ is telling them: You see those massive ruins? You are impressed by what they built? You think they were invincible? They thought so too. They believed their strength and their achievements made them untouchable. Yet where are they now?

The Quraysh, who were building their own sense of pride around their custodianship of the Kaaba and their status in Arabia, needed to understand that no amount of worldly achievement or physical power could protect a people from Allah’s judgment if they failed to maintain their spiritual and moral responsibilities.

Ayah 9

وَثَمُودَ ٱلَّذِينَ جَابُوا۟ ٱلصَّخْرَ بِٱلْوَادِ

And [with] Thamud, who carved out the rocks in the valley

The second nation mentioned is Thamud, descendants of ’Aad and recipients of the message brought by the prophet Salih (AS). Allah ﷻ describes them with a specific characteristic: ٱلَّذِينَ جَابُوا۟ ٱلصَّخْرَ بِٱلْوَادِ – those who carved out rocks in the valley.

The verb jaba means to hollow out, to carve, to dig into something. It’s related to the word for a hole in your pocket or the opening in a soldier’s helmet. Thamud’s distinction was their ability to carve elaborate homes and structures directly into mountains and massive boulders, choosing locations near valleys with water sources and irrigation.

Some archaeologists suggest they built administrative buildings on the ground level while carving their homes high up in the mountains, giving them commanding views and natural fortification. Others indicate they created entire cities within the rock faces themselves. Either way, their skill was extraordinary – they went where others would be afraid to climb and created residences inside solid stone.

The ruins of Thamud can still be seen today in various parts of Arabia, standing as silent testimony to human ingenuity turned toward worldly ambition rather than divine obedience. Like ’Aad before them, they were given a prophet, they were given guidance and they rejected it in favor of their own pride in their achievements.

Ayah 10

وَفِرْعَوْنَ ذِى ٱلْأَوْتَادِ

And [with] Pharaoh, owner of the stakes

The third and climactic example is فِرْعَوْن – Pharaoh – described as ذِى ٱلْأَوْتَادِ, possessor or owner of the stakes/pegs/pillars. This description has been understood in multiple ways by scholars, each revealing something about Pharaoh’s power and cruelty.

ٱلْأَوْتَادِ (singular: وتد) can refer to several things. First, it may reference tent stakes or pegs, indicating that Pharaoh was the first ruler in recorded history to create a standing army that could travel with tents, set up military encampments and then move on to the next location. This was a revolutionary military innovation that gave him tremendous power.

Second, it could refer to the stakes or wooden structures he used for crucifixion. Historical records indicate that anyone who rebelled against Pharaoh’s throne would be nailed to a large wooden frame – similar to a crucifix – and left to die slowly in the sun. This brutal form of execution kept his population in terror.

Third, and most compellingly in the context of this Surah, ٱلْأَوْتَادِ refers to tall structures and buildings – the grand architectural projects for which the Pharaohs became famous. This interpretation is supported by statements from Abdullah ibn Abbas (RA), Mujahid and Al-Hasan Al-Basri, who said it means the owner of great construction.

If Pharaoh was Ramses II (as many scholars believe was the Pharaoh of Musa (AS)), then this description is particularly apt. Ramses II is the most famous of all Pharaohs specifically for his ambitious building projects. Historical records speak of his construction achievements as being on a “Herculean scale.” He employed thousands of workers, spent unprecedented resources and left monuments that inspired awe then and continue to do so today.

In fact, when later civilizations like the Greeks and Romans wanted to demonstrate their own greatness through architecture, they looked to Egyptian columns and structures as their model. To this day, Egyptian architectural motifs (like the pyramid on the US dollar bill) represent the pinnacle of ancient civilization and power.

This is why Allah ﷻ mentions Pharaoh as the climax of these three examples. ’Aad built impressive columns. Thamud carved into mountains. But Pharaoh? Pharaoh built an empire whose architectural legacy became the measuring stick for all subsequent civilizations claiming greatness.

Ayah 11

ٱلَّذِينَ طَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ

[All of] whom oppressed within the lands

Now Allah ﷻ reveals the common thread connecting these three mighty civilizations: ٱلَّذِينَ طَغَوْا۟ فِى ٱلْبِلَـٰدِ– those who transgressed, who rebelled, who oppressed in the lands. The word طَغَوْا۟ doesn’t just mean they rebelled against Allah ﷻ (though that is certainly included). It means they committed great crimes against people.

Here’s the dark reality of these magnificent cities and grand architectural marvels: they were built on the backs of slaves and laborers who were oppressed and treated as less than human. To construct such massive monuments required brutal exploitation of workers. The beautiful exteriors hid ugly oppression within.

This pattern continues throughout history and even today. Cities with the tallest buildings and most impressive infrastructure are often built by workers living in conditions that should shame humanity. The laborers who construct luxury hotels, restaurants and tourist attractions frequently live far from these places in conditions that seem like a different world entirely – sometimes a Third World within a First World nation.

When you see grand, elaborate construction, you must ask: On whose backs was this built? Who suffered so that this could exist? The wealth and beauty on display often mask the theft of dignity and fair wages from those who actually created it.

The people who build such grandiose structures tend to have very high opinions of themselves, which inevitably means they have very low opinions of everyone else. Giving justice to the common people, treating them fairly, ensuring their rights – none of this is on the agenda when a society’s elite become intoxicated with their own greatness.

Ayah 12

فَأَكْثَرُوا۟ فِيهَا ٱلْفَسَادَ

And increased therein the corruption

As a result of their transgression and oppression, these nations فَأَكْثَرُوا۟ فِيهَا ٱلْفَسَادَ– they did too much, they excelled, they were abundant in causing corruption in the land.

This is a profound sociological observation that Allah ﷻ makes repeatedly in the Quran. When nations become extravagant, very wealthy and elaborate, their elite start doing increasingly corrupt things. They throw parties costing millions. They drive cars made of gold. They engage in the most ridiculous frivolities with their wealth, often doing so in the face of the poor who are starving in the same nation.

More disturbingly, the wealthier and more bored these elites become, the more they turn to evil for entertainment. Having experienced every vacation destination, stayed in every luxury hotel and exhausted the normal pleasures of the world, they begin entertaining themselves in increasingly vile, shameless and deplorable ways. The things that entertain the elite of a corrupt society would shock those who don’t have access to their world.

All of this happens at the expense of sucking the blood of the society they rule. They make the poor poorer and more destitute. And to maintain control over a population they are oppressing so severely, they must use force and intimidation – they must keep people under the whip. Because if you oppress a population too much, they will eventually rebel. There will be a groundswell from the bottom, and they will rise up. So the elite must continuously use the whip to maintain control.

Ayah 13

فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ

So your Lord poured upon them a scourge of punishment

The tables turn with this ayah. These nations kept society under a whip, so فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ– your Master unleashed upon them, poured upon them, the whip of His punishment.

The word صَبَّ is particularly powerful. It’s used for water that floods violently into a valley, water that comes and smashes with tremendous force. It describes something that slaps you with overwhelming power. Allah ﷻ chose this word to describe how He whipped these oppressors.

Notice also that it says “your Lord” – رَبُّكَ. Allah ﷻ is directly addressing the Quraysh, reminding them that the same Lord who destroyed these mighty nations is their Lord as well. And He is not just any lord, but a Master who sees oppression and responds to it with justice.

The imagery is especially appropriate for Pharaoh, because how was he killed? He was literally whipped by water – the two mountains of water that came together when the sea closed upon him. He was in the middle of them when they collapsed. The very element he tried to use to kill the children of Israel became the instrument of his own destruction.

Ayah 14

إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِٱلْمِرْصَادِ

Indeed, your Lord is in observation

This is perhaps the most haunting ayah in the passage: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِٱلْمِرْصَادِ– Indeed, your Lord is certainly at the place of ambush. He is ready and waiting at the مِرْصَادِ.

مِرْصَادِ refers to a place where a predator lies in wait. It’s where a lion crouches, watching for prey to come near enough so it can pounce. It’s where a snake hides, camouflaged, waiting for a bird to pass by so it can strike quickly. It’s the vantage point from which you ambush someone.

Allah ﷻ is telling the Quraysh – and through them, telling all of humanity – that He is ready. The weapon is loaded. He is positioned and prepared to strike. You may think you’re getting away with oppression, with transgression, with building your empires on the suffering of others. But your Lord is at the مِرْصَادِ, watching, waiting, and His strike will come.

This is not a threat made in anger. It is a statement of reality, said calmly but with absolute certainty. Your Master is ready to ambush those who oppress and transgress. The question is not if, but when.

Ayah 15

فَأَمَّا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَأَكْرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَكْرَمَنِ

As for man, when his Lord tests him and [thus] is generous to him and favors him, he says, “My Lord has honored me”

Now we arrive at the heart of the Surah, the core message that everything has been building toward. Allah ﷻ shifts from discussing nations to discussing the individual human being – ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ, the human being as a category, as a type.

The structure of this ayah is crucial to understand: فَأَمَّا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ – As for the human being, whenever his Master puts him through a thorough test. The word ٱبْتَلَىٰ means to test thoroughly, to put through trials. And notice: this is where the test begins, right from this point.

Then Allah ﷻ describes what this test looks like: فَأَكْرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُ – so He honors him, He gives him dignity, and He surrounds him with luxury, with blessings, with ease. Let’s pause here because the two words used are distinct and important.

فَأَكْرَمَهُۥ means Allah ﷻ dignifies the person, causes people to respect them, elevates their status in the eyes of others. Maybe He helps you graduate from university and now people look at you differently. Maybe you get a prestigious job and your social standing changes. Maybe your financial situation improves dramatically and suddenly people treat you with more respect. This is ikram – the conferring of dignity and honor.

وَنَعَّمَهُ – and He continuously gives them more and more, getting them accustomed to a better and better life, constantly improving their state. This is the material blessing, the ongoing stream of good things, comfort, ease, luxury.

So there are two components: dignity and material blessings. Allah ﷻ is testing the person by giving them both.

How does the person respond? فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَكْرَمَنِ – so he says, “My Lord has honored me.”

What’s wrong with that response? Allah ﷻ honored you, shouldn’t you acknowledge it? The criticism here is subtle but profound. The person has stopped at acknowledging only the honor. They say, “My Lord has honored me” – full stop. What they fail to say is: “My Lord has honored me AND He is testing me with this honor.”

Notice what Allah ﷻ said at the beginning of the ayah: when He TESTS him by honoring him and blessing him. But the person only sees the honor and blessing. They don’t see the test.

When you say “Allah ﷻ has honored me” and nothing more, there’s an implication that you deserved it. “Allah ﷻ honored me because I’m worthy of honor. Allah ﷻ blessed me because I must be a good person. Allah ﷻ gave me wealth because He must be pleased with me.” You start thinking the blessings are a sign of your own righteousness, your own worthiness.

But if you recognize it as a test, everything changes. Then you ask: What does Allah ﷻ want to see me do with this dignity? Will I dignify others? What does Allah ﷻ want me to do with these blessings? Will I share them with others? Will I use them in His path? Or will I become like the nations before – like ’Aad, Thamud and Pharaoh – who thought their blessings entitled them to oppress others?

Ayah 16

وَأَمَّآ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُۥ فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَهَـٰنَنِ

But when He tests him and restricts his provision, he says, “My Lord has humiliated me”

Now Allah ﷻ presents the flip side: وَأَمَّآ إِذَا مَا ٱبْتَلَىٰهُ – And as for when He tests him (notice, it’s still a test, just a different type). فَقَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُۥ – and He measures out his provision for him, He constrains his provision.

The word قَدَرَ is important. In Arabic, قَدَرَ means exact measure – not more, not less, just precisely what is needed. Allah ﷻ is not saying He gives you less than you need. He’s saying He gives you exactly what you need, or He holds back the extra.

Think about it: the opposite of “too much” isn’t necessarily “not enough.” Allah ﷻ presents it as “exactly right.” But when you’ve become spoiled, when you’ve gotten used to having too much, receiving “just enough” feels like deprivation.

You used to drive a luxury car, now you drive a regular car. You used to wear expensive clothes, now you wear ordinary clothes. You used to eat at restaurants, now you eat simple home cooking. You used to live in a mansion, now you live in an apartment. But you’re still alive. You’re still surviving. Life is still providing for you. Allah ﷻ just restrained it a little bit, brought it back to what you actually need.

How does the person respond? فَيَقُولُ رَبِّىٓ أَهَـٰنَنِ – so he says, “My Lord has humiliated me.”

Not “My Lord is testing me.” Not “My Lord is teaching me something.” Not “My Lord is showing me what really matters.” Instead: “My Lord has humiliated me. Why is this happening to me? Why am I going through this? What did I do to deserve this?”

The parallel structure is deliberate. In the first scenario, Allah ﷻ mentioned two things – أَكْرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُ (honor and material blessings). But in the second scenario, He only mentions one thing – قَدَرَ عَلَيْهِ رِزْقَهُ (measured provision). Why?

Because Allah ﷻ is teaching us something profound: رِزْقَ (provision) is not just money or food or material things. رِزْقَ actually has two components, and the first one mentioned in the previous ayah is dignity. The dignity that your neighbor gives you, the dignity your fellow Muslims give you, the dignity your family gives you, the dignity that society gives you – this is part of your rizq from Allah ﷻ, and it’s actually more important than anything else.

Look at the order: فَأَكْرَمَهُۥ وَنَعَّمَهُ. Dignity comes first, material blessings second. Because if someone is humiliated, it doesn’t matter how much money they have. If someone’s dignity is stripped away, it doesn’t matter what health they enjoy or what possessions they own. Life isn’t worth living without dignity.

Human beings are different from other creatures in this way. Other animals can live without dignity. We cannot. We must have respect. We cannot tolerate humiliation. When Maryam (AS) realized she would face the accusations of her people, she said, “Ya laytani mittu qabla hadha wa-kuntu nasyan mansiyya” – “Oh, I wish I had died before this and been completely forgotten.” She wished she could die before facing the humiliation, and not only die but be completely forgotten – not even a memory of her existence remaining.

This is how deeply humans need dignity. It’s a huge part of our rizq, perhaps the most important part. And then, alongside it, come all the other blessings Allah ﷻ gives us.

Understanding these two ayahs together reveals the central message of the Surah. Allah ﷻ is teaching us that everything in this life – the ups and downs, the gains and losses, the moments of honor and the moments of constraint – all of it is a test. The purpose is to see whether we will remain humble and grateful before Allah ﷻ regardless of our circumstances.

Remember the example of Hajj that opened this Surah? When you go before Allah ﷻ for Hajj, you have no possessions. You have no worldly status. You have no recognition. The only thing you seek is the pleasure of Allah ﷻ. You say, “Ya Allah, whatever You give me, I’m happy with You. You are just. I submit to You completely.” That is the attitude of a believer who has truly succeeded.

But most of humanity doesn’t work like that. We get caught up in the wrappings that Allah ﷻ has placed around us. When times are good, success goes to our heads and we forget Allah ﷻ. When times are difficult, we become depressed and bitter. Our nafs wavers like a pendulum, swinging back and forth between arrogance and despair.

We get consumed by the things we’ve lost – the money we could have made, the status we no longer enjoy, the people who used to like us but don’t anymore. Or we get intoxicated by the things we’ve gained – the recognition, the wealth, the power. All of this makes us think “Allah ﷻ is rewarding me” or “Allah ﷻ is punishing me,” when really Allah ﷻ is testing us through all of it.

The reality is that we are wrapped up in these circumstances like we’re wrapped in clothes. I have a name, an income, a position in society, a family, responsibilities – these are all wrappings around who I truly am. When we stand before Allah ﷻ, all those wrappings will be peeled away. It will just be you, your soul, nothing else. Just you and Allah ﷻ.

But right now, we get so caught up in the wrappings – the good ones and the bad ones – that we fail to see where we truly stand. This is the trial Allah ﷻ gives to humanity. And when enough individuals fail this trial, when enough people in a society start wavering based on their material circumstances rather than remaining steadfast on principle, what happens? Those individuals form nations that look like ’Aad, Thamud and Pharaoh.

You see, the building blocks of any grand construction are individual bricks. We talked about construction throughout this Surah – the columns of ’Aad, the carved mountains of Thamud, the great buildings of Pharaoh. What is the brick of society? It’s the individual human being. It’s the nafs, the soul.

So all of this comes back to fundamental questions: How am I supposed to think about my life? What am I doing? What do I truly owe? Who am I trying to please? What should I be running after? What does contentment really mean? What does being satisfied with what I have actually look like in practice?

If everything around me was gone and I still had Allah ﷻ, would I still be okay? And if everything around me was multiplied many times over, would I be happier just because there’s more there?

Because at the end of the day, there’s only so much food that will go into my mouth. There’s only so much space I need on a bed to sleep. Whatever car I drive, I still just need to go from point A to point B. My basic needs remain my basic needs. I can have a twelve-bedroom house, but I’m only going to sleep in one room. I can have a thousand outfits, but I can only wear one at a time. I can own every gadget and technology, but the cheapest phone and the most expensive phone both send text messages – the function is the same.

This is the realization we must come to. We wrap ourselves up in the blessings Allah ﷻ gives us, or we consume ourselves with desire for things we don’t have, or we grieve over things we used to have. And that becomes all we think about – the material circumstances of our lives. That is the failure of the test. That is what Allah ﷻ is describing when He says “رَبِّيٓ أَكۡرَمَنِ” and “رَبِّيٓ أَهَٰنَنِ.”

To save ourselves from this failure, we must recognize every blessing as a test, every constraint as a test, every circumstance as an opportunity to prove our gratitude and humility before Allah ﷻ. We must see beyond the wrappings to what really matters – our relationship with our Creator, our purpose in this life, our preparation for the next.

May Allah ﷻ preserve all of our dignity, bless us abundantly, make us truly grateful, help us recognize the tests He gives us and grant us success in passing them. May He save us from being like those who think blessings mean they are entitled and constraints mean they are humiliated. May He make us among those who remain content with Him in all circumstances, always saying “Alhamdulillah” – in ease and in hardship, in plenty and in scarcity, knowing that He is testing us through it all.

Ayah 17–30 · linked video: 02. Al-Fajr (Ayah 17-30)

Surah Al-Fajr: Ayahs 17-30

Ayah 17

كَلَّا ۖ بَل لَّا تُكْرِمُونَ ٱلْيَتِيمَ

No, not at all. Allah ﷻ uses the emphatic negation كَلَّا to reject the worldview described in the previous ayahs. This person wavers constantly, measuring their relationship with Allah ﷻ only by what He has given or withheld from them. When things go well, they say their Lord has honored them. When things go poorly, they claim their Lord has disgraced them. But Allah ﷻ diagnoses the real problem: this person has become so self-absorbed, so consumed with their own situation, that they’ve lost sight of those in desperate need around them.

The criticism begins with a profound statement: كَلَّا بَل لَّا تُكْرِمُونَ ٱلْيَتِيمَ - “No, you don’t even honor the orphan.” Notice the choice of words here. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “you don’t feed the orphan” or “you don’t give to the orphan.” He says تُكْرِمُونَ - “you don’t honor.” This is the same root used when the person claimed Allah ﷻ honored or disgraced them. The irony cuts deep: you speak about honor and disgrace from Allah ﷻ, yet you don’t even honor the orphan.

Why is honoring the orphan so significant? In Surah An-Nisa, Allah ﷻ commands: اتَّقُوا اللَّهَ وَاتَّقُوا الْأَرْحَامَ - “Be cautious of Allah and be cautious of the blood relations that come from the womb.” These two types of taqwa are mentioned together because the relationships of the womb are sacred. An orphan is someone whose connection to the womb has been severed through the death of their parents. The loving care that would naturally flow from a mother and father is no longer there. Therefore, your taqwa of Allah ﷻ and your regard for the sacred institution of the womb should compel you to take care of those whose relationship of the womb has been cut.

Immediately after commanding taqwa in Surah An-Nisa, Allah ﷻ says: وَآتُوا الْيَتَامَىٰ أَمْوَالَهُمْ - “Give the orphans their wealth.” Why are orphans mentioned first? Because taking care of those who’ve lost their blood support is an expression of taqwa itself. This is part of honoring the religion of Allah ﷻ.

The Quraysh positioned themselves as custodians of the Sacred House, dedicated to religious affairs. But true dedication to Allah ﷻ has two inseparable components: devotion to Him and service to humanity. The Quraysh maintained a facade of religious devotion while failing their responsibility to those in need. When you’re in charge of Allah’s sacred house, that means you serve both Allah ﷻ and His creation.

Ayah 18

وَلَا تَحَـٰٓضُّونَ عَلَىٰ طَعَامِ ٱلْمِسْكِينِ

The criticism deepens. Not only do they fail to honor the orphan, they don’t even encourage one another to feed the poor. The word تَحَـٰٓضُّونَ means to incite others, to stir their emotions, to motivate them toward action. They don’t motivate each other because whoever speaks up will have fingers pointed back at them: “What have you contributed?” If they haven’t contributed themselves and don’t want to, there’s an unspoken agreement—nobody talks about it, and therefore no expectations fall on anyone.

The linguistic precision here is remarkable. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say طَعَامَ الْمِسْكِينِ - “feeding the poor.” He says طَعَامِ الْمِسْكِينِ - “the food of the poor.” When you feed someone, you’re giving them from what you have. But when you speak of “the food of the poor,” you’re acknowledging that the food belongs to them to begin with. You’re not feeding them—you’re giving them what is rightfully theirs. This completely reframes the act of charity. You never get to feel like you’ve done anyone a favor. You’re fulfilling their right, returning to them what was always meant to be theirs.

This brings us back to the concept of honoring. You honor those in better positions than yourself—students honor teachers, followers honor their prophets ﷺ, younger people honor elders. Why should the orphan and the poor be honored? Because these are gifts from Allah ﷻ to us. They are means by which we earn His forgiveness. We should go out of our way to honor them.

The word مِسْكِينِ itself carries deep meaning. It’s actually a composite of two roots: مَسَكَ meaning “to be stuck” and سَكَنَ meaning “to be still or motionless.” A miskeen is someone stuck in a situation they cannot escape—a taxi driver who loses his sight and can no longer work, a writer whose hands no longer function, an artist who can’t practice their craft anymore. These are people trapped in circumstances beyond their control.

How do you identify such people? You have to know them intimately. You have to understand their situation from the inside. This word contains within it the responsibility to know the poor in our society, to mingle with them, to understand their struggles. The institution of prayer facilitates this. In Salah, we stand in rows without checking anyone’s income. Whether someone walked to the masjid, drove, or biked, they’re all in one row together. When we say Salaam to our left and right, we’re acknowledging each other. The purpose of congregational prayer is for families to know one another, to understand each other’s situations.

The larger purpose extends to Hajj, where people from all walks of life gather as equals. But this requires us to look—to actually see people. The Quran describes: يَحْسَبُهُمُ الْجَاهِلُ أَغْنِيَاءَ مِنَ التَّعَفُّفِ تَعْرِفُهُم بِسِيمَاهُمْ - “The ignorant one thinks they have no need because they don’t beg; you’ll recognize them by the marks on their faces” (2:273). You have to look at people, notice when someone seems stressed, and ask: “Is everything okay? Let’s sit and talk.”

Why would someone be so caring about others? Only when they’re not lost in themselves. When all you think about is your own situation—رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ or رَبِّي أَهَانَنِ—you’re so consumed with your own world that you don’t have time to look at the face of the person next to you.

Ayah 19

وَتَأْكُلُونَ ٱلتُّرَاثَ أَكْلًا لَّمًّا

Moving from strangers in the community—the orphans and the poor—Allah ﷻ now addresses what happens within families. وَتَأْكُلُونَ ٱلتُّرَاثَ - “You consume the inheritance.” The word أَكْلًا - “eating” - is significant. When you eat something, it’s gone completely, consumed entirely with nothing left. It can’t be returned. This is different from simply taking something, which might be given back.

What Allah ﷻ describes here was common in pre-Islamic Arab culture: when inheritance was distributed, the stronger family members—older brothers, sons as opposed to mothers or sisters—would quickly seize everything and either hide it, invest it, or use it up before anyone else could touch it. They would devour it so completely that it couldn’t be recovered.

Remarkably, this expression of “eating inheritance” was not used in Arab literature before the Quran. Allah ﷻ introduced this phrase to capture something specific about how viciously people approached inheritance. The criticism intensifies with أَكْلًا لَّمًّا - you eat it by gathering together. The word لَمَّ comes from the root meaning to gather or collect, even small things—like gathering hair into a ponytail. When Allah ﷻ says أَكْلًا لَّمًّا, it’s like someone so hungry they’re eating and then gathering up even the crumbs, unwilling to let a single piece go to anyone else.

This ayah came in Makkah, well before the detailed inheritance laws were revealed in Surah Al-Baqarah and other Medinan surahs. Before Allah ﷻ could establish what percentage goes to whom, He had to address the fundamental problem: greed. Even in Muslim families today, when inheritance issues arise, we quote Shariah much later. First comes the question: “What am I going to get?” The interpretation of Islamic law gets twisted so someone can get more and someone else gets less.

Brothers in different countries fight over property. Those in the home country say, “We’re the ones here, we’ll keep it.” Those abroad say, “We can afford better lawyers.” Everyone’s involved—spouses, in-laws—all fighting. And they have the audacity to claim they’re following Allah’s book or the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ, when their only consideration is: consume, get more, get more, get more.

Ayah 20

وَتُحِبُّونَ ٱلْمَالَ حُبًّا جَمًّا

This ayah explains everything that came before it. Why don’t you honor the orphan? Why don’t you encourage feeding the poor? Why do you consume inheritance so viciously? وَتُحِبُّونَ ٱلْمَالَ - “You love wealth.” That alone is devastating criticism. But Allah ﷻ adds: حُبًّا جَمًّا - “an intense love,” a love that has gathered and overrun inside you.

The word جَمَّ is used when water overflows from a well—when something accumulates beyond its boundaries. Your heart has become so filled with love of wealth that nothing else fits. It has overrun you completely. You’ve become so consumed with money that it dominates every thought, every interaction.

There are people and families who only think about money constantly. The only time they’ll give you attention or say Salaam is when money is involved. Some parents become so focused on wealth that they demand their children’s paychecks be deposited in joint accounts so they can monitor the funds, making sure the daughter-in-law doesn’t get anything. Some children become so consumed they neglect their parents’ needs entirely.

This is وَتُحِبُّونَ ٱلْمَالَ حُبًّا جَمًّا. In this love of wealth, you don’t care whose rights are denied or who gets trampled. This greed makes a person turbulent in their life—never at peace, always chasing more.

This is Allah’s criticism of the Quraysh. Notice what’s remarkable here: Allah ﷻ doesn’t criticize shirk directly. He doesn’t criticize disbelief. The person even acknowledges Allah ﷻ—they say “my Lord honored me” or “my Lord disgraced me.” The acknowledgment of Allah ﷻ isn’t the problem. The problem is how they treat people.

Remember the beginning of the surah, where Allah ﷻ described great nations that rebelled and caused corruption—إِرَمَ ذَاتِ الْعِمَادِ and others who oppressed their people and enslaved them. Now He’s saying: you’re no different. They did that on a grand scale with great power, and you’re doing this. They had massive architecture and armies, and Allah ﷻ is about to show that all of it means nothing.

Ayah 21

كَلَّآ إِذَا دُكَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا

كَلَّا - No, all of this will come to an end. إِذَا دُكَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا - “When the earth is crushed, pounded over and over again.” The word دَكَّ means to crush soil, to flatten something completely, to grind it into powder. The repetition—دَكًّا دَكًّا—emphasizes the totality: crushed thoroughly, flattened completely, over and over until nothing remains.

This connects directly to the earlier part of the surah. We saw grand architecture—إِرَمَ ذَاتِ الْعِمَادِ (Iram of the pillars), ثَمُودَ الَّذِينَ جَابُوا الصَّخْرَ بِالْوَادِ (Thamud who carved rock in the valley), فِرْعَوْنَ ذِي الْأَوْتَادِ (Firaun of the stakes). Massive structures, great buildings, impressive monuments. And now Allah ﷻ says: the entire earth will be crushed to powder. All that architecture—gone. And if that’s gone, what will be left of you?

The crushing of the earth also means something else: when the earth is flattened completely, there’s no place to hide. No castle, no fortress, no shelter. You’re completely exposed. In military terms, the most vulnerable position is on flat land. You need the cover of trees, mountains, rocks—something to hide behind for strategic positioning. When the earth is crushed flat, there’s no strategic position left. You’re entirely exposed, completely helpless.

Ayah 22

وَجَآءَ رَبُّكَ وَٱلْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا

The earth has been flattened, leaving everyone exposed. Now: وَجَآءَ رَبُّكَ وَٱلْمَلَكُ صَفًّا صَفًّا - “And your Lord comes, and the angels in rows upon rows.” The angels descend in ranks—صَفًّا صَفًّا. Some scholars say these rows represent the seven ranks of angels corresponding to the seven heavens. Just as earthly militaries have hierarchies—foot soldiers, lieutenants, commanders—the angels have ranks. As you ascend through the heavens, the classification of angels becomes higher and higher. On Judgment Day, they descend by rank, rows upon rows.

This is a direct contrast to the powerful nations mentioned earlier. We spoke of Firaun with his mighty armies, of Thamud with their strength. Allah ﷻ says: that’s nothing. All of what they built will be crushed. Let Me show you a real army—the army of angels descending. The military of the heavens makes every earthly power look like nothing.

Ayah 23

وَجِا۟ىٓءَ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِجَهَنَّمَ ۚ يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ

وَجِا۟ىٓءَ يَوْمَئِذٍۭ بِجَهَنَّمَ - “And on that day, Hell itself will be brought forward.” This perplexed classical scholars. What does it mean Hell is brought forward? It suggests Hell already exists—it’s not being created that day, but exposed, revealed. Some classical authorities believed Hell exists beneath our feet right now, and Allah ﷻ will tear the earth open and Hell will emerge. Whether that’s the case, Allah ﷻ knows best, but the language indicates Hell’s existence before Judgment Day.

يَوْمَئِذٍ يَتَذَكَّرُ ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ - “On that day, the human being will remember.” Full memory returns. Sharp, complete recollection. Earlier in the surah, Allah ﷻ described people who only remembered themselves, so consumed with their own situation they couldn’t think about those in need around them. But when Hell comes forward, when they see it, memory becomes crystal clear. Every crime, every mistake, every act of neglect—they remember it all. The Quran says elsewhere: لَّقَدْ كُنتَ فِي غَفْلَةٍ مِّنْ هَـٰذَا فَكَشَفْنَا عَنكَ غِطَاءَكَ فَبَصَرُكَ الْيَوْمَ حَدِيدٌ - “You were in heedlessness of this, so We’ve removed your cover, and your sight today is sharp” (50:22).

وَأَنَّىٰ لَهُ ٱلذِّكْرَىٰ - “But how could that remembrance possibly benefit him?” The word أَنَّىٰ is an emphatic form of “how”—stronger than just كَيْفَ. How in the world? What’s the point of remembering now? What’s the point of repenting now?

Ayah 24

يَقُولُ يَـٰلَيْتَنِى قَدَّمْتُ لِحَيَاتِى

The person keeps saying—يَقُولُ suggests repeated utterance—يَـٰلَيْتَنِى. This expression is difficult to translate perfectly. يَا لَيْتَ is used when you’re regretful, overwhelmed with remorse, at a loss for words. In English, we might just say “Oh my God!” or make sounds of distress because we don’t even have words. Ancient languages had specific expressions for these moments—Shakespeare’s “Woe is me!” comes close, though we’d never naturally say that today.

The point is: this person is utterly distraught, beyond words. يَـٰلَيْتَنِى قَدَّمْتُ لِحَيَاتِى - “If only I had sent ahead for my life,” or “If only I had made my life a priority.” The word قَدَّمْتُ means to send ahead, to prioritize. “If only I had prioritized my life properly.”

Wait—wasn’t this person’s entire problem that they only thought about themselves? That’s all they ever prioritized! Why are they saying this? Because they thought about their temporary life and never thought about their permanent life. When Hell emerges, they realize: the real life was always ahead, in the hereafter. The Quran says: وَإِنَّ الدَّارَ الْآخِرَةَ لَهِيَ الْحَيَوَانُ - “The final home, that is the actual life” (29:64). لَوْ كَانُوا يَعْلَمُونَ - “If only they knew.”

These same greedy people wouldn’t spend money because they needed savings, security, retirement funds, real estate investments, business capital. They always thought about their future—but only their worldly future. Allah ﷻ says: Ironically, you never did think about your future. You never thought about your actual life. And now you’re crying that you neglected your real future.

Ayah 25

فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُعَذِّبُ عَذَابَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ

Allah ﷻ wraps up the criminal’s regret with punishment: فَيَوْمَئِذٍ لَّا يُعَذِّبُ عَذَابَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ - “On that day, no one can torture the way He tortures.” Nobody will experience the kind of torment Allah ﷻ is capable of inflicting.

Why is He saying this? Look back: the surah mentioned oppressive nations, then oppressive individuals who dismissed the needy in their society. Allah ﷻ says: You think you know oppression? You think you can punish? You think you understand torture? Let Me show you torture like nothing you’ve ever seen. Nobody can torture the way your Lord can.

Ayah 26

وَلَا يُوثِقُ وَثَاقَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ

وَلَا يُوثِقُ وَثَاقَهُۥٓ أَحَدٌ - “And no one can bind the way He binds.” The word وَثَاق means tight binding—chains, shackles, restraints. It’s used for securing an animal or capturing a prisoner of war.

This surah is about the nafs—the self that’s unleashed, uncontrolled, reckless, consuming whatever it wants, caring only about itself. That same nafs, that same person who felt so free to do whatever they pleased, will be chained, bound, imprisoned, restrained like never before. These were people of power—everyone else was under their control, under their oppression. Now the tables turn completely. Nobody has been tied up tighter. Nobody will chain and imprison like Allah ﷻ does. Nobody will arrest in a way comparable to Him.

Ayah 27

يَـٰٓأَيَّتُهَا ٱلنَّفْسُ ٱلْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ

Now we reach one of the most beautiful passages in the entire Quran, describing the relationship between us and Allah ﷻ that will happen on the Day of Resurrection—but which can actually begin even now.

Notice where we are in the sequence. The first part of the surah was about human beings struggling to come close to Allah ﷻ through the ritual of Hajj. The middle part described the turbulent nafs—all over the place, self-indulgent, not having found Allah ﷻ yet. And now this final section describes those who have attained tranquility. The first was an attempt to attain tranquility in this world through Hajj; this last section is having attained that tranquility when we meet Allah ﷻ. May Allah ﷻ make us all among them.

يَـٰٓأَيَّتُهَا - Allah ﷻ is calling out, but in the most dignified way. He doesn’t just say يَا نَفْسُ. The ايَّتُهَا is an extension done specifically to honor the one being addressed, to give them even more dignity. When Allah ﷻ speaks on Judgment Day, everyone listens. The هَا is meant to capture attention. But He goes out of His way to capture the attention of this particular nafs, even though He doesn’t need to. He doesn’t say يَا مَعْشَرَ الْجِنِّ وَالْإِنسِ (O gathering of jinn and humans) like He does elsewhere. Here He addresses individually: يَـٰٓأَيَّتُهَا ٱلنَّفْسُ - O you, this soul, this person. He dignifies and honors them.

ٱلْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ - “the tranquil one,” “the one at peace.” This is a powerful word. The root ط م ن implies something that was meant to rise or move being deliberately calmed down. Like when a child is hyperventilating and you do something to settle them. Or when milk is boiling over and you turn off the stove and it calms down—that’s طُمَأْنِينَة.

Allah ﷻ describes the nafs as something that constantly wants to erupt. It has temptations, desires, urges, hungers, appetites always waiting to explode. The nafs exists between two forces: on one side, the body (جَسَد) with its animal needs and wants, constantly pulling, demanding—I want to eat, I want to look at that, I want to say this, I want this money, this job, this place. The body is essentially an animal, and it keeps pulling.

On the other side is the روح - the soul or spirit that Allah ﷻ breathed into us. This rooh already knows Allah ﷻ, is inclined toward Him, made of light. When revelation comes—which is also called light—it feeds the rooh. The rooh tells the nafs: “You can have some of what the body wants, but don’t violate your limits. Don’t do that.”

So the nafs is this mediator, this thing in between. Sometimes it caves to the body’s pressure and commits sin. When it does, it feels bad. Where does that bad feeling come from? The rooh has been torn, violated slightly. The nafs starts feeling guilty—this is النَّفْسُ اللَّوَّامَةُ, the self-blaming soul. That feeling of guilt is actually your rooh crying, a trigger telling you something is wrong and needs to be fixed.

People around the world—not just Muslims, all human beings—feel guilt. How do they make up for it? Some do terrible things but give huge amounts to charity, thinking it balances out. Shaitan beautifies their deeds: “So long as I do this good stuff, I’m alright.” It gives temporary peace, but they keep cycling back to evil.

Allah ﷻ doesn’t want the nafs to erupt in evil, then compensate with good, then erupt again, then compensate again. He wants النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ - calm, balanced, at peace. Allah ﷻ gave us desires and urges, but He also gave us this deen, this guidance, to balance them. Give your rooh what it needs, give your body what it needs, and balance the two. Navigate both, and you’ll find النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ.

The word نَفْس itself is related to نَفَس - breath. Inhale, exhale. Two sides, both needing to be addressed: the physical and the spiritual. Both must be honored.

Ayah 28

ٱرْجِعِىٓ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَّرْضِيَّةً

ٱرْجِعِىٓ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ - “Return to your Lord.” Our journey began when Allah ﷻ created us. Before we were born, when we were souls, Allah ﷻ asked: أَلَسْتُ بِرَبِّكُمْ - “Am I not your Lord?” And we said: بَلَىٰ - “Yes, of course You are.” Then our souls were placed in our mothers’ wombs. We were born, lived this life, our nafs went through all kinds of experiences—and at the end of it all, Allah ﷻ says: come back. Come back to where it all began. ٱرْجِعِىٓ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ.

We’ve left everything else behind. You don’t take your home, your money, your clothes, anything. It’s just you and Allah ﷻ. That’s all there is. When He calls you and you’ve left things behind, normally you’d worry about what you left—I miss home, I miss this. Children traveling say “I miss this toy, I miss that.” We don’t want to leave loved ones, families, homes. But when Allah ﷻ calls, He describes you as completely content—no dissatisfaction, no disturbance whatsoever.

رَاضِيَةً - “pleased, content, happy.” You’re happy that you’re returning to Allah ﷻ, experiencing joy beyond anything you’ve ever known. One scholar beautifully said: “The final meeting is when you return to your Lord, and He is the One who makes you laugh and makes you cry.” When we meet Allah ﷻ, the joy will make us laugh, and the overwhelming feeling will make us cry—both at the same time.

Then Allah ﷻ adds: مَّرْضِيَّةً - “well-pleased with.” Just because you’re happy with Allah ﷻ doesn’t mean He’s happy with you. So He adds this: there is contentment directed toward you. Allah ﷻ is happy with you. The angels are happy with you. The Prophet ﷺ is happy with you. Ibrahim (AS) is happy to meet you. Your father Adam (AS) is happy to meet you. You’re in the company of people who are all pleased with you.

We love being in the company of those content with us. It’s disturbing and depressing to be around those who despise us or look down on us. Allah ﷻ says: مَّرْضِيَّةً - first and foremost, your Lord Himself is happy with you.

This is profound. Sometimes people spend their whole lives seeking acknowledgment from their fathers. No matter what they accomplish, the father isn’t proud. But when that father finally says “I’m proud of you, son”—he didn’t give money, didn’t give inheritance, just said those words—the grown man breaks down crying. Some people’s acknowledgment means everything.

We didn’t know if Allah ﷻ was happy with us. We made mistakes, disappointed Him plenty. Now He’s calling us toward Himself. We’re happy He’s calling—but is He happy with us? مَّرْضِيَّةً - He tells you He’s pleased with you. That couldn’t make you any happier.

The word مَّرْضِيَّةً also means “to be made happy” - the passive form, meaning you’re given joy. Both meanings work together. The fact that Allah ﷻ acknowledges He’s pleased with you—there’s no greater joy than that. مَرْضِيَّةً - given supreme joy.

Ayah 29

فَٱدْخُلِى فِى عِبَـٰدِى

فَٱدْخُلِى فِى عِبَـٰدِى - “Enter among My slaves.” Come, let Me introduce you. These are My عِبَاد - My devoted servants. Now you get introduced to those Allah ﷻ loves, those who were also called by Him—all the believers, the prophets, the great followers. You’ll sit crying next to Musa (AS): “I recited about you, I read about you!” You’ll be in the company of those who drank from the hand of the Prophet ﷺ at the Hawd al-Kawthar.

Look at this: entering the highest place in Jannah, and the term used is عِبَاد - slaves. In worldly terms, slave is the lowest designation a human being can have. No title or role goes lower than slave. Yet when you become a slave of Allah ﷻ, this lowest worldly term becomes the highest possible honor. There is no higher title than عَبْد - slave of Allah ﷻ.

If you just become His slave—give up your pride, ego, greed, submit yourself completely—see what ranks He raises you to. That’s why in the Quran, whenever Allah ﷻ mentions elevating the prophets ﷺ, He always uses عَبْد (abd), never رَسُول (rasool): سُبْحَانَ الَّذِي أَسْرَىٰ بِعَبْدِهِ - “Glory be to Him who took His slave by night” (17:1). The highest station a human being can reach—even for the prophets ﷺ—is عَبْد.

We are proud to be عِبَاد. Some say “slave” isn’t a good term, we should use “servant.” No—slave is better because it’s more humiliating, has less dignity, and we want to bring no dignity before Allah ﷻ except what He grants us. We want complete and absolute humility before Him. The term is only problematic because we think of slavery to other humans. To Allah ﷻ, there is no greater honor.

Also, servant implies limited service—certain hours, certain job descriptions. An accountant serving a company isn’t expected to mop floors. Service has boundaries. When is a slave a slave? Always. What’s a slave’s job description? Anything the master says. That’s us before Allah ﷻ. We’re not just servants—we’re slaves, honored to be slaves.

Ayah 30

وَٱدْخُلِى جَنَّتِى

وَٱدْخُلِى جَنَّتِى - “And enter My Garden.” Notice the sequence. The first thing mentioned: you’re pleased with Allah ﷻ, and you come to Him. That’s the greatest gift in the akhirah—the company of Allah ﷻ, meeting Him, knowing He’s happy with you and you’re happy with Him. That’s first.

The second greatest gift: فَٱدْخُلِى فِى عِبَـٰدِى - you get to meet others who preceded you, those also made happy by Allah ﷻ. Meeting the believers, the prophets ﷺ, the righteous.

The third item: وَٱدْخُلِى جَنَّتِى - enter My Garden. The palaces, rivers, spouses, trees, drinks, food—all of that comes third. First was Allah ﷻ, then His slaves, then—and only then—these material blessings.

This is Allah’s way of teaching us something crucial: you can have the greatest wealth in this world. You can have rivers, gardens, artificial waterfalls in your backyard, palaces, beautiful spouses, luxury cars, every comfort that Jannah describes—you can get versions of it here. But if Allah’s company isn’t there, if the company of the righteous isn’t there, none of that means anything. That’s not Jannah.

The material blessings are mentioned last deliberately. They only have meaning when the first two are in place: nearness to Allah ﷻ and companionship with His righteous slaves.

Reflection on the Surah’s Structure

This surah is divided into five distinct sections, forming a beautiful symmetry:

Section 1 (Opening): Human beings struggling to attain closeness to Allah ﷻ through the ritual of Hajj, attempting to find tranquility. We leave ourselves behind, our bodily needs, wanting only to please Allah ﷻ and revive our rooh in its purest form. The call of لَبَّيْكَ اللَّهُمَّ لَبَّيْكَ - “Here I am, O Allah, here I am” - is our response to His invitation.

Section 2: Historical nations with great power and architecture who were destroyed because they used their strength to oppress others.

Section 3 (Middle): The turbulent nafs—wavering, self-indulgent, consumed with itself, unable to see those in need. This person hasn’t found Allah ﷻ yet, vacillating between رَبِّي أَكْرَمَنِ and رَبِّي أَهَانَنِ.

Section 4: The failure of the Quraysh as a nation—not through grand oppression like the historical peoples, but through being indulgent toward themselves while dismissive of the orphan, the miskeen, consuming inheritance viciously, loving wealth excessively.

Section 5 (Conclusion): Those who have attained النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ—the tranquil soul—and their meeting with Allah ﷻ. This is the successful culmination of what Section 1 was attempting.

Sections 1, 3, and 5 all relate to the nafs: attempting to control it through Hajj, the turbulence when it’s uncontrolled, and finally achieving tranquility when meeting Allah ﷻ.

Sections 2 and 4 describe the failure of nations: historical peoples with their grand crimes, and the Quraysh with their personal greed affecting their society.

The message is clear: if individuals don’t control their nafs, societies head toward destruction. The oppression of great nations and the self-absorption of the Quraysh both lead to the same end. Allah ﷻ will crush all earthly power—دُكَّتِ ٱلْأَرْضُ دَكًّا دَكًّا. The punishment mentioned for historical nations in Section 2 (فَصَبَّ عَلَيْهِمْ رَبُّكَ سَوْطَ عَذَابٍ - “Your Lord poured upon them a scourging punishment”) finds its ultimate fulfillment in Section 4: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ - “Your Lord is lying in wait.” That final reckoning is described in the middle sections with the crushing of the earth and the descent of angels.

May Allah ﷻ make us people of النَّفْسُ الْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ. May He overlook our mistakes and make us among those sensitive to the needs of others around us. May He grant us the honor of hearing يَـٰٓأَيَّتُهَا ٱلنَّفْسُ ٱلْمُطْمَئِنَّةُ ٱرْجِعِىٓ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكِ رَاضِيَةً مَّرْضِيَّةً when we meet Him.

Ayah 1–16 · linked video: 01. Al-Fajr (Ayah 1-16)

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 1

الْفَجْرِ (al-fajr) [ف-ج-ر]

The root ف-ج-ر carries the meaning of explosion or bursting forth. In its primary usage, it refers to rocks that burst open to gush water out. When applied to dawn, fajr captures the imagery of darkness being torn open or exploding with light. This is not merely sunrise but the dramatic breaking of night’s darkness as light forces its way through. The word conveys violence and sudden appearance rather than gradual illumination, emphasizing the power of this daily transformation.

Ayah 2

لَيَالٍ (layalin) [ل-ي-ل]

The root ل-ي-ل refers to night or darkness. The plural form layalin (nights) is used here without specification, creating interpretive openness. The word itself carries connotations of time passing, struggle, and the period when believers are tested most severely. Nights in Arabic usage often represent periods of difficulty or spiritual exertion.

عَشْرٍ (ashrin) [ع-ش-ر]

The root ع-ش-ر means ten. This is a definite number but without specification of which ten nights, creating scholarly debate. The use of a specific number rather than a general term suggests these are particular, significant nights known to the original audience through their cultural or religious context.

Ayah 3

الشَّفْعِ (ash-shaf’i) [ش-ف-ع]

The root ش-ف-ع means to pair, to make even, or to join together. Shaf’ refers to even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8, etc.) or things that come in pairs. The definite article ‘al’ suggests these are specific even numbers or pairs, not just any even concept. In the context of hajj rituals, this could reference the even days or paired aspects of worship.

الْوَتْرِ (al-watr) [و-ت-ر]

The root و-ت-ر means singular, odd, or alone. Watr refers to odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7, 9) or that which stands alone without a pair. The contrast with shaf’ creates a comprehensive pairing that encompasses all numbers. In hajj context, this particularly highlights the ninth night of Dhul-Hijjah (Arafat), an odd-numbered yet supremely significant night.

Ayah 4

اللَّيْلِ (al-layl) [ل-ي-ل]

The root ل-ي-ل again refers to night. Here it is definite (the night) rather than indefinite, suggesting a specific night known to the audience. The specification distinguishes this from the ten nights mentioned earlier.

يَسْرِ (yasri) [س-ر-ي]

The root س-ر-ي means to travel at night, to flow, to penetrate, or to pass through quickly. The verb yasri captures the image of something moving swiftly and penetrating through space. This can describe how quickly a significant night passes, or how it seems to flow through and disappear. The root also carries meanings of a spear penetrating armor, suggesting the piercing quality of time’s passage.

Ayah 5

قَسَمٌ (qasam) [ق-س-م]

The root ق-س-م means to swear, to take an oath, or to divide. Qasam specifically refers to a solemn oath or sworn statement. The use of this word confirms that the preceding verses are indeed divine oaths, giving them tremendous weight and significance.

حِجْرٍ (hijr) [ح-ج-ر]

The root ح-ج-ر means to prevent, restrain, prohibit, or create a barrier. Hijr refers to the intellect specifically because the mind’s function is to restrain base desires, temper, greed, and lust. It creates barriers against unbridled passion. This word shares linguistic origins with hajj (pilgrimage), as both involve restraint and control. The word emphasizes intellectual capacity to exercise self-discipline and moral judgment.

Ayah 6

رَبُّكَ (rabbuka) [ر-ب-ب]

The root ر-ب-ب means to nurture, sustain, raise, or be master over. Rabb is the One who owns, sustains, and has complete authority. The possessive ‘ka’ (your) makes this personal and direct, emphasizing the intimate relationship between the listener and their Lord.

فَعَلَ (fa’ala) [ف-ع-ل]

The root ف-ع-ل means to do or to act. This is the simple past tense, indicating completed action. The question form ‘how did your Rabb deal’ invites reflection on historical events as lessons.

عَادٍ (Aad) [ع-و-د]

Aad is the proper name of an ancient Arab nation. The name itself has become synonymous with transgression and rebellion in Arabic usage.

Ayah 7

إِرَمَ (Iram) [ء-ر-م]

Iram could be the name of their ancestor, their capital city, their greatest king, or their elite ruling tribe (analogous to Quraysh in Mecca). The lack of definitive meaning in Quran preserves multiple historical interpretations.

الْعِمَادِ (al-imad) [ع-م-د]

The root ع-م-د means columns, pillars, or supports. Imad refers to massive architectural columns or structural supports. This emphasizes their advanced construction capabilities and architectural achievements. It can also metaphorically refer to their physical stature as they were described as unusually tall and strong people, like human pillars.

Ayah 8

يُخْلَقْ (yukhlaq) [خ-ل-ق]

The root خ-ل-ق means to create or bring into existence. The passive form yukhlaq means ‘was created’ or ‘were created.’ This emphasizes Allah’s creative power and the uniqueness of what He created in the nation of Aad.

مِثْلُهَا (mithluhā) [م-ث-ل]

The root م-ث-ل means like, similar to, or equivalent. Mithl means ‘the likes of’ or ‘something similar to.’ The negation ‘lam yukhlaq mithluhā’ (the likes of which were never created) emphasizes their unprecedented nature in both architectural achievement and physical capability.

الْبِلَادِ (al-bilād) [ب-ل-د]

The root ب-ل-د means land, country, or territory. Bilād is the plural, referring to various lands or countries. This emphasizes the scope of the comparison: nowhere in any land had such a civilization appeared.

Ayah 9

ثَمُودَ (Thamud) [ث-م-د]

Thamud is the proper name of another ancient Arab nation, descendants of Aad. They became infamous for their rejection of Prophet Salih (AS) and their arrogance.

جَابُوا (jābu) [ج-و-ب]

The root ج-و-ب means to dig, carve, excavate, or hollow out. Jābu (they carved/excavated) describes their unique skill in carving homes and structures into mountains and boulders. The root also refers to making a hole or opening, like a hole in a pocket (jayb) or the opening of a helmet.

الصَّخْرَ (as-sakhr) [ص-خ-ر]

The root ص-خ-ر means rock, boulder, or hard stone. Sakhr specifically refers to large, solid rocks or boulders, emphasizing the difficulty and impressiveness of their architectural feat.

بِالْوَادِ (bil-wād) [و-د-ي]

The root و-د-ي means valley or low-lying area between mountains. Wād refers to valleys with water sources, making them desirable locations. They strategically chose valleys with water and irrigation, then carved their homes into the surrounding mountains.

Ayah 10

فِرْعَوْنَ (Fir’awn) [ف-ر-ع]

Fir’awn (Pharaoh) is the title of Egyptian kings. The root suggests elevation, being high, or branching out like a tall tree. This connects to their arrogance and elevated status.

الْأَوْتَادِ (al-awtād) [و-ت-د]

The root و-ت-د means peg, stake, or tent peg driven into ground. Awtād is the plural. This could refer to: (1) military tent pegs for the first organized standing army that established military encampments, (2) stakes or wooden posts used for crucifixion and torture of rebels, or (3) metaphorically, the massive architectural pillars and structures that Pharaoh built, making him the owner of great constructions. The third meaning fits best with the Surah’s architectural theme and historical records of Ramses II’s unprecedented building projects.

Ayah 11

طَغَوْا (tagħaw) [ط-غ-ي]

The root ط-غ-ي means to transgress, exceed limits, rebel, or become tyrannical. Ṭaghaw (they transgressed) indicates they crossed all boundaries of justice and morality. This is not just rebellion against Allah but crimes against humanity.

أَكْثَرُوا (aktharū) [ك-ث-ر]

The root ك-ث-ر means to be many, abundant, or numerous. Aktharū means they made abundant or they multiplied. This emphasizes they didn’t just commit corruption, but excelled in it, made it abundant throughout their lands.

الْفَسَادَ (al-fasād) [ف-س-د]

The root ف-س-د means corruption, decay, mischief, or moral degradation. Fasād encompasses all forms of corruption: social, moral, economic, and political. It includes oppression of laborers, exploitation of the poor, extravagance of the wealthy, and shameless entertainment of the elite at society’s expense.

Ayah 12

صَبَّ (ṣabba) [ص-ب-ب]

The root ص-ب-ب means to pour out, pour down, or flow forcefully. Ṣabba specifically describes water flooding and smashing into a valley with tremendous force. This metaphorically describes how Allah’s punishment came down upon them with overwhelming power. The word choice is particularly appropriate for Pharaoh who was destroyed by water crashing together.

سَوْطَ (sawṭ) [س-و-ط]

The root س-و-ط means whip or lash. Sawṭ is the whip used for punishment and control. Since these nations kept their populations under control through oppressive whipping and punishment, Allah turned that same instrument back on them metaphorically. The whip they used on others became the whip of divine punishment upon them.

عَذَابٍ (adhāb) [ع-ذ-ب]

The root ع-ذ-ب means punishment, torment, or suffering. Adhāb is severe punishment, particularly that which is painful and humiliating. The word emphasizes the painful nature of their consequences.

Ayah 13

لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ (la-bil-mirṣād) [ر-ص-د]

The root ر-ص-د means to watch, observe, lie in wait, or ambush. Mirṣād is the place of ambush where a predator (like a lion or snake) waits camouflaged, ready to pounce when prey comes close enough. This conveys Allah’s readiness and His perfect timing in bringing justice. He is not unaware or distant, but positioned and ready like a hunter in ambush, waiting for the right moment to execute judgment.

Ayah 14

الْإِنسَانُ (al-insān) [ء-ن-س]

The root ء-ن-س relates to humans, mankind, or being human. Insān is the human being, emphasizing our shared human condition and psychological patterns that Allah is describing.

ابْتَلَاهُ (ibtalāhu) [ب-ل-و]

The root ب-ل-و means to test, try, or put through trial. Ibtalā is to thoroughly test someone, to put them through a complete examination. This is not casual testing but comprehensive trial that reveals character. The connection to Ibrahim (AS) who passed tests before being honored with hajj responsibilities is significant here.

أَكْرَمَهُ (akramahu) [ك-ر-م]

The root ك-ر-م means to honor, dignify, or be generous to. Akrama means to bestow honor and dignity upon someone. This is the first part of Allah’s test through blessing: giving someone honor and respect in society, elevation in status.

نَعَّمَهُ (na’amahu) [ن-ع-م]

The root ن-ع-م means to bless, give comfort, or provide luxury. Na’ama (Form II) means to continuously shower with blessings, to surround with luxury and comfort, to keep improving someone’s state with more and more ease and material good. This is the second part of Allah’s test: material blessings, wealth, and comfort beyond honor alone.

Ayah 15

قَدَرَ (qadara) [ق-د-ر]

The root ق-د-ر means to measure, decree, or proportion precisely. Qadara means to give exact measure, nothing more and nothing less. Significantly, Allah doesn’t say He withholds or gives less than needed. Rather, He gives precisely what is needed but holds back the excess that one became accustomed to. This is not deprivation but exact provision, though it feels like constraint to someone spoiled by abundance.

رِزْقَهُ (rizqahu) [ر-ز-ق]

The root ر-ز-ق means provision, sustenance, or that which Allah provides. Rizq encompasses all provisions: food, wealth, health, dignity, respect, and blessings. Earlier in Ayah 14, rizq was defined as both akramahu (dignity/honor from others) and na’amahu (material blessings), with dignity mentioned first, indicating honor and respect are primary components of human provision.

أَهَانَنِ (ahānani) [ه-و-ن]

The root ه-و-ن means to humiliate, disgrace, or make light of. Ahāna means to humiliate or strip of dignity. The human response ‘my Rabb has humiliated me’ reveals the flawed perception that reduced material blessings equal divine humiliation, when in reality it may simply be constrained provision as a test.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayahs 1-4

The opening four ayahs are structured as a series of divine oaths (qasam), each introduced by the particle ‘wa’ (and/by). This creates a rhythmic, building intensity as oath is layered upon oath. In Arabic rhetoric, when Allah swears by His creation, it elevates the significance of both what is sworn by and what will follow. The sequence begins with fajr (singular, definite), then layālin ashr (plural, indefinite in one sense but defined by number), then shaf’ and watr (both definite with ‘al’), and concludes with layl (singular, definite). This movement from singular to plural to paired concepts to singular again creates a complete rhetorical arc.

The verb yasri (يَسْرِ) in Ayah 4 is particularly notable. It is a present tense verb that conveys continuous action - ‘that passes’ or ‘that penetrates through’ - suggesting ongoing movement and the fleeting nature of time. The choice of this verb over a simple past tense emphasizes the active, dynamic quality of this night’s passage.

Ayah 5

The rhetorical question ‘hal fi dhālika qasamun li-dhi ḥijr’ (هَلْ فِي ذَٰلِكَ قَسَمٌ لِّذِي حِجْرٍ) employs interrogative style not to seek information but to create emphasis and provoke reflection. The ‘hal’ question format here is rhetorical, meaning ‘surely there is’ rather than ‘is there?’ The phrase ‘li-dhi ḥijr’ (for those of restraint/intellect) specifically addresses those capable of understanding, creating an implicit criticism of the Quraysh who possessed this ritual tradition but failed to grasp its meaning.

The demonstrative ‘dhālika’ (that) points back to all four oaths collectively, binding them together as a single unit of meaning worthy of reflection.

Ayah 6

The sudden shift from divine oaths to direct address ‘a-lam tara kayfa fa’ala rabbuka’ (أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ) creates rhetorical impact. The interrogative ‘a-lam’ (أَلَمْ - have you not?) expects an affirmative answer, making it a rhetorical question that assumes the listener’s awareness. The verb ‘tara’ (see/perceive) is significant: it calls for observation and reflection, not just intellectual knowledge.

The possessive pronoun ‘rabbuka’ (your Rabb) is used throughout this passage rather than Allah or other divine names. This choice emphasizes the personal relationship between the Quraysh and their Lord, and highlights their responsibility to Him who has authority over them.

Ayah 7

The descriptive phrase ‘Irama dhāt al-’imād’ employs the feminine ‘dhāt’ (possessor of) which is used for prominent, defining characteristics. This construction emphasizes that columns/pillars were THE defining feature of Iram, not just one characteristic among many.

Ayah 8

The passive construction ‘allatī lam yukhlaq mithluhā’ (which was not created the like of it) emphasizes Allah as the Creator while focusing attention on the creation itself. The relative pronoun ‘allatī’ (which - feminine) agrees with Iram, maintaining grammatical connection. The perfect tense verb ‘yukhlaq’ with the negation particle ‘lam’ emphasizes the completed, definitive nature of this uniqueness: never before, never since.

Ayah 9

The phrase ‘wa Thamūda alladhīna jābū al-ṣakhra bil-wād’ uses the masculine plural relative pronoun ‘alladhīna’ to describe Thamud, emphasizing them as a people/collective. The verb ‘jābū’ (they carved/hollowed) is followed by the direct object ‘al-ṣakhr’ (the rock) with the preposition ‘bi’ attached to ‘al-wād’ (in the valley), creating a clear picture of location and action.

Ayah 10

The construct phrase ‘Fir’awna dhū al-awtād’ uses the masculine ‘dhū’ (possessor of) rather than the feminine ‘dhāt,’ matching Pharaoh’s gender. The definite article on ‘awtād’ (the pegs/stakes) suggests these were well-known, perhaps famous aspects of Pharaonic power.

Ayah 11

The coordinated verbs ‘ṭaghaw… fa-aktharū’ (they transgressed… then they multiplied) show progression: first transgression, then multiplication of corruption. The ‘fa’ (then/so) indicates consequence and sequence. The verb ‘aktharū’ (they made abundant) is followed by the direct object ‘al-fasād’ (the corruption) with no preposition, making corruption the direct, immediate result of their actions.

Ayah 12

The dramatic verb ‘fa-ṣabba ’alayhim rabbuka sawṭa ’adhāb’ employs powerful imagery. The verb ‘ṣabba’ (poured down/unleashed) is used for violent flooding, creating a metaphor of punishment as overwhelming flood. The construct phrase ‘sawṭa ’adhāb’ (whip of punishment) places ‘sawṭ’ in the construct state, making it inseparable from ‘adhāb’ - the whip IS punishment incarnate.

The particle ‘fa’ at the beginning indicates consequence: because of what preceded (their transgression and corruption), therefore Allah unleashed punishment.

Ayah 13

The nominal sentence ‘inna rabbaka la-bil-mirṣād’ (إِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَبِالْمِرْصَادِ) employs multiple emphasis particles. ‘Inna’ (إِنَّ) emphasizes the entire statement. The ‘la’ (لَ) before ‘bil-mirṣād’ adds additional emphasis - this is emphatic certainty. The preposition ‘bi’ attached to ‘al-mirṣād’ indicates not just presence AT the place of ambush but readiness and preparedness IN that position. This is not passive waiting but active readiness to strike.

The definite article ‘al’ on ‘mirṣād’ may suggest THE place of ambush (a specific known place) or emphasize the concept of ambush itself. Either way, it conveys readiness and certainty.

Ayah 14

The structure ‘fa-ammā al-insānu idhā mā ibtalāhu rabbuhu…’ employs the emphatic particle ‘fa-ammā’ (as for) to introduce a new subject with emphasis. The ‘idhā mā’ (whenever) creates a conditional temporal clause indicating repeated pattern, not a single incident. This grammatical choice indicates Allah is describing universal human tendency, not one person’s story.

The verbs ‘fa-akramahu wa-na’amahu’ are coordinated with ‘wa’ (and), showing two simultaneous divine actions. Both are in the perfect tense with third person masculine singular object pronoun ‘hu’ (him), maintaining grammatical agreement.

The human response ‘fa-yaqūlu rabbī akraman’ uses ‘fa’ to show immediate consequence - ‘then he says’ - indicating the person’s instant interpretation. The verb form of ‘akrama’ here is actually a past tense verb used as if it’s something Allah did (‘my Rabb honored me’), showing the person attributes this to Allah’s assessment of their worth. There is no mention of test in their response, revealing their failure to understand.

Ayah 15

The parallel structure ‘wa-ammā idhā mā ibtalāhu fa-qadara ’alayhi rizqahu…’ mirrors Ayah 14 exactly, creating balance and comparison. The same conditional temporal ‘idhā mā’ again indicates repeated pattern.

However, the verbs shift: instead of ‘akrama wa-na’ama,’ we have ‘qadara ’alayhi rizqahu’ (he measured precisely upon him his provision). The preposition ‘’alayhi’ (upon him) adds nuance - this measuring/constraining is something imposed upon the person, felt as restriction.

The response ‘fa-yaqūlu rabbī ahānan’ again uses ‘fa’ for immediate reaction. The verb ‘ahāna’ (he humiliated me) is the human’s interpretation, contrasting with the divine action of ‘qadara’ (measured precisely). Allah doesn’t say He humiliated, but the human interprets constraint as humiliation, revealing flawed perception.

Ayah 16 (Note: Ayah 16 was not covered in this transcript, so no analysis for it is included here)

No specific content on this ayah in this lecture.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayahs 1-4

The opening oaths reference the hajj, an institution that predates Islam and traces back to Prophet Ibrahim (AS). The Arabs of Mecca, particularly Quraysh, were custodians of this ritual for generations.
